abstract: The performance of large language model (LLM) systems depends not only on model weights, but also on their harness: the code that determines what information to store, retrieve, and present to the model. Yet harnesses are still designed largely by hand, and existing text optimizers are poorly matched to this setting because they compress feedback too aggressively. We introduce Meta-Harness, an outer-loop system that searches over harness code for LLM applications. It uses an agentic proposer that accesses the source code, scores, and execution traces of all prior candidates through a filesystem. On online text classification, Meta-Harness improves over a state-of-the-art context management system by 7.7 points while using 4x fewer context tokens. On retrieval-augmented math reasoning, a single discovered harness improves accuracy on 200 IMO-level problems by 4.7 points on average across five held-out models. On agentic coding, discovered harnesses surpass the best hand-engineered baselines on TerminalBench-2. Together, these results show that richer access to prior experience can enable automated harness engineering.

citations.fullCitation: Qizheng Zhang, Changran Hu, Shubhangi Upasani, Boyuan Ma, Fenglu Hong, V. Kamanuru, Jay Rainton, Chen Wu, Mengmeng Ji, Hanchen Li, Urmish Thakker, James Zou, and K. Olukotun. Agentic context engineering: Evolving contexts for self-improving language models. In arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.04618, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Agentic Context Engineering (ACE), a state-of-the-art, hand-designed harness for online text classification. ACE serves as a crucial baseline in the main paper's experiments, where Meta-Harness is shown to significantly improve accuracy by 7.7 points while using 4x fewer context tokens.
citations.title: Agentic context engineering: Evolving contexts for self-improving language models

citations.fullCitation: Alexander Novikov, Ng ˆan V ˜u, Marvin Eisenberger, Emilien Dupont, Po-Sen Huang, Adam Zsolt Wagner, Sergey Shirobokov, Borislav Kozlovskii, Francisco JR Ruiz, Abbas Mehrabian, et al. Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.13131, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper represents a key class of prior work on program search, where an LLM acts as a mutation operator. Meta-Harness contrasts its approach of providing full, unstructured access to prior experience against methods like AlphaEvolve, which use more structured and limited feedback, and shows superior performance in direct comparisons.
citations.title: Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

citations.fullCitation: Mert Yuksekgonul, Daniel Koceja, Xinhao Li, Federico Bianchi, Jed McCaleb, Xiaolong Wang, Jan Kautz, Yejin Choi, James Zou, Carlos Guestrin, et al. Learning to discover at test time. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.16175, 2026.
citations.justification: This work introduces TTT-Discover, a text-optimization method used as a direct experimental baseline. The comparison highlights the core contribution of Meta-Harness, which outperforms TTT-Discover by providing the proposer with full filesystem access to raw execution traces rather than more compressed or structured feedback.
citations.title: Learning to discover at test time

citations.fullCitation: Mike A Merrill, Alexander G Shaw, Nicholas Carlini, Boxuan Li, Harsh Raj, Ivan Bercovich, Lin Shi, Jeong Yeon Shin, Thomas Walshe, E Kelly Buchanan, et al. Terminal-bench: Benchmarking agents on hard, realistic tasks in command line interfaces. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.11868, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the TerminalBench-2 benchmark, which is used for one of the three main experimental evaluations of Meta-Harness. The ability of Meta-Harness to automatically discover a harness that achieves state-of-the-art performance on this difficult and actively contested benchmark is a key result demonstrating its practical value for complex agentic tasks.
citations.title: Terminalbench: Benchmarking agents on hard, realistic tasks in command line interfaces

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Meta-Harness: End-to-End Optimization of Model Harnesses

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "Meta-Harness: End-to-End Optimization of Model Harnesses" was authored by:
*   Yoonho Lee (Stanford)
*   Roshen Nair (Stanford)
*   Qizheng Zhang (Stanford)
*   Kangwook Lee (KRAFTON)
*   Omar Khattab (MIT)
*   Chelsea Finn (Stanford)

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The performance of large language model (LLM) systems is influenced not only by the underlying model weights but also by the "harness"—the external code that dictates how information is stored, retrieved, and presented to the model. Historically, the design and refinement of these harnesses, often referred to as harness engineering, has largely been a manual process, relying on human practitioners to inspect failures, adjust heuristics, and iterate on designs. This manual approach can lead to significant performance discrepancies, with harnesses potentially causing up to a 6x difference in model performance on the same benchmark.

This work addresses a gap in automated LLM system development by proposing an end-to-end optimization method for harnesses. Existing text optimization methods, which iteratively improve textual artifacts based on feedback, are generally poorly suited for complex harness engineering. These methods typically suffer from aggressive feedback compression, operating on limited context (e.g., conditioning only on scalar scores, restricting feedback to short templates or summaries, or processing only recent history). This compression often discards crucial information required to diagnose and address long-horizon failures that are characteristic of stateful harness logic. For instance, prior text optimizers typically handle contexts ranging from hundreds to tens of thousands of tokens per iteration, whereas harness optimization often requires diagnostic information orders of magnitude larger.

Meta-Harness positions itself at the intersection of several research areas, including credit assignment, meta-learning, adaptive access to external context, executable code search, and text optimization. Unlike traditional meta-learning which updates model weights, Meta-Harness assigns credit at the harness level, reasoning about components responsible for failures and rewriting external code. It extends concepts from retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and memory-based agents, which adaptively access external knowledge, by applying similar adaptive access patterns to optimize the *context-management procedures themselves*. In contrast to executable code search methods that evolve functions within fixed scaffolds or search predefined workflow graphs, Meta-Harness performs search over full harness implementations, including prompt construction, retrieval, and state update strategies, providing unrestricted filesystem access to prior experience. Its key distinction from earlier text optimizers lies in its capacity to access and process the full, uncompressed history of prior candidates' source code, execution traces, and evaluation scores, enabling more sophisticated causal reasoning and targeted edits that are not feasible with limited feedback.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to automate the process of harness engineering, which has traditionally been a manual and iterative human endeavor. The motivation for this automation stems from several observations:

1.  **Significant Performance Impact of Harnesses:** The introduction highlights that the harness, which governs how an LLM interacts with its environment, can lead to substantial performance variations. A single model's output can vary by a factor of six depending on its harness, underscoring the criticality of effective harness design.
2.  **Labor-Intensive Manual Engineering:** Current practice involves human practitioners manually refining prompts, context-management rules, and tool-use logic. This process is time-consuming, heuristic-driven, and relies heavily on human intuition and repeated inspection of failures. Automating this could reduce development cycles and enhance system robustness.
3.  **Limitations of Existing Automated Methods:** Existing text optimization techniques are inadequate for harness engineering. Harnesses are stateful programs with long-horizon effects; a decision made early in an interaction can significantly impact outcomes many steps later. Current optimizers provide "compressed" or "short-horizon" feedback (e.g., scalar scores, brief summaries, or limited context windows of past attempts), which is insufficient for diagnosing complex failures distributed across code, scores, and execution traces. This lossy feedback often removes the critical diagnostic information needed to link downstream failures to specific upstream harness design choices.
4.  **Need for Richer Feedback Mechanisms:** To overcome these limitations, the authors aim to develop a system that can leverage rich, uncompressed historical data. The motivation is to enable an intelligent agent to selectively inspect raw prior code, detailed execution traces, and comprehensive evaluation scores, thereby allowing it to form causal hypotheses about failures and propose more informed and targeted modifications to the harness code.

In essence, the research seeks to develop an end-to-end search system, Meta-Harness, capable of autonomously discovering high-performing LLM harnesses by providing its proposing agent with full, unsummarized access to all past experimental experience.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

Meta-Harness is designed as an outer-loop optimization procedure that searches over task-specific harnesses. The core principle is to allow an agentic proposer to selectively inspect comprehensive prior experience, including code and execution traces, via a filesystem, rather than relying on condensed summaries.

**Harness Definition and Objective:**
A harness is conceptualized as a stateful program that encapsulates an LLM, managing the context the model perceives at each step. Given a fixed language model $M$ and a task distribution $X$, the harness $H$ produces a rollout trajectory $\tau$. A task-specific reward function $r(\tau, x)$ scores this trajectory. The optimization objective is to find $H^*$ that maximizes the expected reward: $H^* = \text{arg max}_H E_{x \sim X, \tau \sim p_M(H,x)} r(\tau, x)$. When multiple objectives (e.g., accuracy and context cost) are relevant, the system evaluates candidates based on Pareto dominance.

**Meta-Harness Search Loop (Algorithm 1, Figure 2):**
The system employs a single coding-agent proposer with access to a growing filesystem $D$, which functions as its feedback channel. The search process iterates as follows:

1.  **Initialization:** A population of initial valid harnesses $H$ is provided, and an empty filesystem $D$ is initialized.
2.  **Initial Evaluation:** Each harness in the initial population is evaluated, and its code, evaluation scores, and execution traces are stored in $D$.
3.  **Iterative Optimization:** For a fixed number of iterations ($N$):
    *   **Proposer Query:** The proposer, implemented as a coding agent (e.g., Claude Code with Opus-4.6), queries the filesystem $D$. It inspects the source code, evaluation scores, and detailed execution traces of all prior candidate harnesses. This inspection is performed using standard terminal tools like `grep` and `cat`, as the full history often exceeds the proposer's context window.
    *   **Harness Proposal:** Based on its inspection and reasoning about likely failure modes, the proposer generates $k$ new harness candidates. The proposer is responsible for deciding what information to inspect and how to modify the code, rather than adhering to hard-coded search heuristics.
    *   **Validation and Evaluation:** Each newly proposed harness undergoes interface validation. If valid, it is evaluated on the search set (a subset of task instances used for feedback).
    *   **Logging:** All logs (the proposed code, the proposer's reasoning traces, and the evaluation scores) for the new harness are added to the filesystem $D$.
4.  **Final Output:** After $N$ iterations, the Pareto frontier of harnesses stored in $D$ is returned.

**Key Design Principles:**

*   **Filesystem-based Full History Access:** For every previous candidate harness, the filesystem stores its complete source code, evaluation scores, and detailed execution traces (prompts, tool calls, model outputs, state updates). This allows the proposer to selectively access granular diagnostic information. A single evaluation can produce up to 10 million tokens of diagnostic data, vastly exceeding typical feedback budgets of prior methods.
*   **Agentic Proposer:** The proposer is a language-model-based system capable of invoking developer tools and modifying code. It functions as an autonomous coding agent, determining which prior artifacts to inspect, which failure modes to address, and the scope of modifications.
*   **Code-Space Search:** Optimization occurs directly in the code space, enabling algorithmic modifications to retrieval, memory, or prompt-construction logic, including full program rewrites. This provides a natural regularization bias, as coding models tend to propose coherent algorithms.

**Practical Implementation Details:**

*   Harnesses are implemented as single-file Python programs modifying task-specific logic.
*   The proposer LLM (Claude Code with Opus-4.6) is guided by a minimal domain-specific skill. The base LLM ($M$) for the tasks is frozen and varies by domain.
*   Typical runs involve approximately 60 harness evaluations over 20 iterations. Test-set results are not visible to the proposer during search.

**Experimental Setup:**
Meta-Harness was evaluated across three diverse domains:

1.  **Online Text Classification:** Uses GPT-OSS-120B. Evaluated on LawBench, Symptom2Disease (S2D), and USPTO-50k. Comparisons against human-designed (zero-shot, few-shot, ACE, MCE) and program-search (Best-of-N, OpenEvolve, TTT-Discover, GEPA) methods. Ablation studies tested the impact of different feedback levels (scores-only, scores-plus-summary, full Meta-Harness). OOD generalization was assessed on nine unseen datasets.
2.  **Retrieval-Augmented Math Reasoning:** Optimizes retrieval policies for IMO-level math problems. Uses a corpus of over 500,000 deduplicated and decontaminated solved problems. Search performed on a 250-problem set using GPT-OSS-20B. Evaluated on 200 unseen IMO-level problems across five held-out models (GPT-5.4n, GPT-5.4m, Gem-3.1FL, Gem-3F, GPT-20B). Baselines include no retrieval, dense retrieval, random few-shot, and BM25 retrieval.
3.  **Agentic Coding on TerminalBench-2:** Optimizes harnesses for LLM agents on 89 challenging long-horizon tasks. Search initialized from Terminus 2 and Terminus-KIRA. Evaluated on TerminalBench-2 itself, using Claude Opus 4.6 and Claude Haiku 4.5 as base models, comparing against leaderboard agents.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of Meta-Harness across three distinct domains demonstrated its capacity to discover high-performing harnesses.

**Online Text Classification:**
*   **Performance and Efficiency:** Meta-Harness achieved 48.6% accuracy, outperforming the state-of-the-art hand-designed Agentic Context Engineering (ACE) by 7.7 points (from 40.9%) and Meta Context Engineering (MCE) by 8.6 points. This improvement was realized while using 4x fewer context tokens (11.4K vs. 50.8K for ACE), indicating increased efficiency.
*   **Comparison with Text Optimizers:** When compared to existing text optimizers, Meta-Harness matched the final accuracy of OpenEvolve and TTT-Discover within approximately 4 evaluations, a 10x reduction in evaluation budget. Its final accuracy surpassed these methods by over 10 points (56.7% best vs. 43.3% for OpenEvolve and 45.6% for TTT-Discover on search set).
*   **Importance of Raw Traces:** An ablation study showed that providing the proposer with full access to raw execution traces was critical. The full Meta-Harness interface achieved a median accuracy of 50.0% and best accuracy of 56.7%, significantly outperforming the "scores-only" (34.6% median, 41.3% best) and "scores-plus-summary" (34.9% median, 38.7% best) conditions.
*   **Accuracy-Context Tradeoffs:** Meta-Harness was able to discover a Pareto frontier of harnesses, demonstrating a controlled trade-off between accuracy and context cost.
*   **Out-of-Distribution Generalization:** The discovered harnesses generalized to nine unseen, diverse classification datasets. Meta-Harness achieved an average accuracy of 73.1%, outperforming ACE (70.2%) and all few-shot baselines, and demonstrated the highest performance on 6 out of 9 datasets. Two representative discovered harnesses, "Draft Verification" and "Label-Primed Query," exhibit distinct strategies for memory-based context construction (Appendix B.1).

**Retrieval-Augmented Math Reasoning:**
*   **Cross-Model Performance:** A single retrieval harness discovered by Meta-Harness improved accuracy on 200 IMO-level math problems by an average of 4.7 points across five held-out models (GPT-5.4n, GPT-5.4m, Gemini-3.1FL, Gemini-3F, GPT-20B), compared to the "No Retriever" baseline (38.8% vs. 34.1%).
*   **Baseline Comparison:** This harness outperformed or matched strong fixed baselines, achieving a 1.3-point average gain over BM25 retrieval and avoiding performance regressions observed with dense retrieval or random few-shot prompting across several models. The discovered harness is a compact four-route BM25 program that routes queries lexically to subject-specific retrieval policies, incorporating deduplication and difficulty reranking (Appendix B.2).

**Agentic Coding on TerminalBench-2:**
*   **Competitive Benchmark Performance:** Meta-Harness automatically discovered harnesses that achieved competitive performance on TerminalBench-2.
    *   With Claude Opus 4.6, it attained a 76.4% pass rate, surpassing the hand-engineered Terminus-KIRA (74.7%) and ranking #2 among all reported Opus 4.6 agents on the leaderboard.
    *   With Claude Haiku 4.5, it achieved 37.6%, outperforming the next-best reported agent (Goose, 35.5%) by 2.1 points and ranking #1 among all Haiku 4.5 agents.
*   **Qualitative Proposer Behavior:** Analysis of the search trajectory revealed that the proposer engaged in causal reasoning. For instance, it identified that early regressions were confounded by shared prompt interventions, isolated structural bugfixes from prompt rewrites, and subsequently pivoted towards safer, additive modifications, such as "environment bootstrapping" (Appendix A.2, B.3). This additive modification gathers a snapshot of the sandbox environment before the agent loop begins, injecting it into the initial prompt to eliminate early exploratory turns, particularly beneficial for tasks requiring domain-specific tooling.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Meta-Harness research demonstrates that automated, end-to-end optimization of LLM harnesses is feasible and can yield substantial performance improvements over manually engineered solutions and existing text optimization methods.

**Primary Significance:**

*   **Advancing Automated Harness Engineering:** The work provides evidence that the largely manual and heuristic-driven process of designing LLM harnesses can be effectively automated. By enabling an agent to search and refine harness code directly, Meta-Harness moves beyond fixed templates or summary-based feedback, opening avenues for more robust and efficient LLM system development.
*   **Superior Performance and Efficiency:** The consistently superior performance of Meta-Harness-discovered harnesses across diverse and challenging tasks (text classification, math reasoning, agentic coding), often with greater context efficiency, indicates a significant advancement in LLM system optimization.
*   **Generalizability and Transferability:** The discovered harnesses exhibited generalization capabilities to unseen datasets and base models, suggesting that the optimization process can uncover broadly effective strategies rather than merely overfitting to specific search environments. These strategies are also codified as readable Python programs, making them transferable and reusable.
*   **Importance of Rich, Selective Feedback:** The ablation studies highlight that rich, uncompressed access to full historical diagnostic information (raw code, execution traces, scores) is crucial. This contrasts with prior methods that rely on lossy summaries and scalar rewards, underscoring the necessity for detailed feedback to enable causal reasoning and targeted modifications in complex code-space search.

**Potential Impact:**

*   **Accelerated LLM Application Development:** By automating harness engineering, Meta-Harness could significantly reduce the development time and expertise required to deploy high-performing LLM-powered applications.
*   **Enhanced LLM Capabilities:** Discovered harnesses can unlock new capabilities for LLMs, such as improved long-horizon reasoning and complex task completion, as demonstrated in the math reasoning and agentic coding domains.
*   **New Research Avenues:** The findings suggest that providing powerful coding agents with access to structured historical data could be a general paradigm for automating other complex software engineering tasks. Future work could explore co-evolution of harnesses and model weights, allowing strategy and learning to mutually inform each other, or investigate the performance of Meta-Harness with a broader range of proposer agents.
*   **Improved Transparency and Debuggability:** Since optimization occurs in the code space, any discovered "overfitting" or brittle logic is inspectable, offering a level of transparency that is difficult to achieve with weight-space optimization in neural networks. This inspectability can build confidence in the robustness of automated solutions.

In conclusion, Meta-Harness represents a step towards automating complex, real-world LLM engineering challenges, leveraging the capabilities of advanced coding agents and structured access to comprehensive experiential data.
overview:
The performance of large language model (LLM) systems is defined not only by the parameters of the underlying model but also by the "harness"—the external software environment that manages how information is retrieved, stored, and presented to the model. While significant research effort is dedicated to optimizing model weights, the development of these harnesses remains largely a manual, trial-and-error process. Manual harness engineering involves human practitioners inspecting failure cases, adjusting prompts, and refining context-management heuristics.

Recent studies suggest that for the same underlying model, the design of the harness can result in up to a six-fold difference in performance on standard benchmarks. This gap highlights the criticality of harness design and the potential for automated optimization. However, current automated text and prompt optimization methods often struggle with the complexity of harnesses. These systems are typically "stateful" programs where decisions made early in an interaction (such as what to store in memory) have long-horizon consequences for later steps. Traditional optimizers, which rely on compressed feedback like scalar scores or short summaries, often lose the diagnostic granularity needed to identify and fix these complex, multi-step failures.

## The Meta-Harness Framework: Agentic Search in Code-Space

Meta-Harness is an end-to-end optimization system that automates the discovery of high-performing harnesses. It treats the harness as a stateful program that encapsulates an LLM and manages the context the model perceives at each step. 

![Meta-Harness System Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.28052v1/x3.png)
*Figure 1: The Meta-Harness optimization loop. An agentic proposer (1) generates harness code by inspecting a filesystem of prior experience. The candidate is evaluated on tasks (2), and its full execution logs, including reasoning traces and scores, are stored back into the filesystem (3) for future iterations.*

Formally, given a fixed language model $M$ and a task distribution $X$, a harness $H$ produces a rollout trajectory $\tau$. The system aims to find an optimal harness $H^*$ that maximizes the expected reward $r(\tau, x)$ across the task distribution:

$$
H^* = \arg \max_H \mathbb{E}_{x \sim X, \tau \sim p_M(H,x)} [r(\tau, x)]
$$

Unlike previous methods that optimize over prompt strings or limited templates, Meta-Harness searches directly in the space of executable code. The core innovation lies in its feedback mechanism: instead of providing the optimizer with a "compressed" summary of past performance, Meta-Harness gives a coding agent (the proposer) selective access to a full, uncompressed filesystem $D$ containing all prior candidate code, evaluation scores, and detailed execution traces.

The optimization process follows an iterative loop:
1.  **Search History Access:** The proposer, an LLM-based coding agent, uses standard terminal tools (e.g., `grep`, `cat`) to browse the filesystem $D$. This allows the agent to inspect the raw prompts, model outputs, and tool-call sequences that led to specific failures in previous versions.
2.  **Harness Proposal:** Based on its reasoning, the agent proposes $k$ new harness candidates. Because the search happens in code-space, the agent can introduce entirely new logic for retrieval, state updates, or prompt construction.
3.  **Evaluation and Logging:** Each new harness is evaluated on a subset of task instances. Critically, every bit of diagnostic data—up to millions of tokens per evaluation—is logged to the filesystem.
4.  **Refinement:** This cycle repeats for $N$ iterations, building a comprehensive database of "what worked and what didn't," allowing the proposer to form and test causal hypotheses about harness design.

## The Role of Uncompressed History

A central finding of this research is the importance of providing the proposer with "uncompressed" historical data. Most existing optimizers operate under strict context constraints, often providing the proposer with only the most recent results or a brief summary of failures. In contrast, Meta-Harness treats the entire history of the search as a browsable knowledge base. 

The necessity of this approach is evident when dealing with harnesses that manage memory. A failure at step 50 of a task might be caused by a suboptimal storage decision at step 2. A scalar score ("0/1") or a high-level summary ("model failed to find information") is insufficient to diagnose that the specific data point was never stored in the harness's state. By allowing the proposer to inspect the raw execution trace $\tau_x$ for a specific instance $x \sim X$, Meta-Harness enables the agent to identify the exact line of code responsible for the upstream failure.

![Harness Optimizer Search Progress](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.28052v1/x5.png)
*Figure 2: Search progress comparison in the online text classification domain. Meta-Harness reaches superior performance significantly faster than existing text optimizers like OpenEvolve or TTT-Discover, which use more restricted feedback channels.*

Ablation studies conducted by the authors confirmed this: providing only scores or human-written summaries of failures resulted in significantly lower final performance compared to providing the agent with raw execution logs. The agent's ability to selectively "deep-dive" into specific traces is what allows it to navigate the vast search space of executable code effectively.

## Evaluating Meta-Harness Across Diverse Domains

To test the generality of the approach, the authors evaluated Meta-Harness across three distinct domains: online text classification, math reasoning, and agentic coding.

### Online Text Classification
In the classification domain, the harness must decide which previous examples to keep in the model's context to help classify new incoming data. The search was performed using a 120B parameter open-source model. Meta-Harness discovered strategies that outperformed existing hand-engineered methods like Agentic Context Engineering (ACE). 

![Accuracy vs Context Trade-off](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.28052v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: The Pareto frontier discovered by Meta-Harness in text classification. The system successfully identifies multiple harnesses that balance high accuracy with low context cost (measured in characters), outperforming human-designed baselines.*

Notably, the discovered harnesses generalized to nine unseen datasets. One discovered strategy, termed "Label-Primed Query," used a two-step process: first, the model identifies the most likely label, and then it specifically retrieves examples corresponding to that label to confirm the decision. This algorithmic refinement was discovered entirely through automated search and achieved 48.6% accuracy, surpassing ACE's 40.9% while using four times fewer context tokens.

### Retrieval-Augmented Math Reasoning
For IMO-level (International Mathematical Olympiad) math problems, the harness manages a retrieval system to find relevant solved problems from a corpus of 500,000 examples. Meta-Harness discovered a retrieval policy that improved accuracy across five different held-out base models by an average of 4.7 points. The discovered harness was a compact program that routed queries to subject-specific retrieval policies based on the problem's text, incorporating deduplication and difficulty-based reranking.

### Agentic Coding on TerminalBench-2
The most complex domain tested was agentic coding, where an LLM must interact with a terminal to solve software engineering tasks. Starting from a strong human-written baseline, Meta-Harness optimized the harness's environment-handling and prompt-construction logic.

![TerminalBench-2 Performance](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.28052v1/x2.png)
*Figure 4: Pass rates on TerminalBench-2. Meta-Harness discovered harnesses that allowed Claude models to achieve state-of-the-art performance on the leaderboard, surpassing specialized human-engineered agents.*

On TerminalBench-2, the discovered harness for Claude Haiku 4.5 achieved a 37.6% pass rate, ranking #1 among Haiku-based agents. For the more powerful Claude Opus 4.6, it achieved 76.4%, ranking #2 on the overall leaderboard. The proposer identified that the agent often wasted initial turns exploring the environment. To fix this, the proposer added an "environment bootstrapping" step that automatically snapshots the sandbox environment and injects it into the initial prompt, saving exploratory turns and improving success rates.

## Analysis: Why Meta-Harness Succeeds

The success of Meta-Harness can be attributed to several factors that distinguish it from prior work in meta-learning and text optimization:

1.  **Code-Space Search as Regularization:** Optimizing in the space of Python programs provides a natural bias toward coherent, algorithmic solutions. Unlike weight-space optimization, which can be opaque, the resulting harnesses are human-readable and modular.
2.  **Causal Reasoning via Logs:** By providing the proposer with the ability to "debug" previous attempts using raw logs, the system moves from random or heuristic search to informed, causal reasoning. The agent doesn't just know *that* a harness failed; it can see *how* it failed.
3.  **End-to-End Credit Assignment:** Meta-Harness assigns credit at the level of the harness logic. It can recognize when a prompt change is insufficient and a structural change to the retrieval or state-management code is required.
4.  **Multi-Objective Discovery:** Because the system evaluates many candidates, it naturally identifies a Pareto frontier of solutions, allowing developers to choose harnesses that optimize for different constraints, such as maximizing accuracy versus minimizing inference cost.

## Conclusion and Implications

Meta-Harness demonstrates that the labor-intensive process of harness engineering can be effectively automated through agentic search over executable code. By shifting the focus from manual heuristic tuning to automated search over full execution histories, the framework consistently finds strategies that outperform human-designed counterparts across varied domains.

The implications of this work extend beyond LLM benchmarks. The ability of an agent to autonomously refine its own external "scaffolding" by reasoning over its past experience suggests a path toward more self-improving AI systems. Furthermore, because the output of Meta-Harness is transparent Python code, these discovered strategies can be easily inspected, audited, and deployed in production environments, providing a bridge between automated optimization and reliable software engineering. The transition from "prompt engineering" to "automated harness engineering" represents a significant shift in how complex LLM systems are developed and scaled.

keyInsights: Full, uncompressed access to historical diagnostic data (raw code, execution traces, and evaluation scores) is critical for an agent to perform causal reasoning and targeted modifications in complex, stateful code-space search.

Direct optimization in the code space, enabled by an agentic proposer, facilitates algorithmic modifications and complete program rewrites, providing a natural regularization bias towards coherent solutions.
An agent, when provided with rich, granular feedback, can effectively identify and address complex, long-horizon failures in stateful programs, going beyond fixed templates or summary-based feedback.
originalProblem: Harness engineering, which designs how large language models (LLMs) store, retrieve, and present information, is a largely manual, iterative process relying on human intuition and heuristics.
The performance of LLM systems is highly sensitive to harness design, with variations potentially causing up to a 6x difference in model performance on the same benchmark.

Existing text optimization methods are inadequate for complex harness logic due to aggressive feedback compression and limited context, which discards crucial information needed to diagnose long-horizon failures.
results: Meta-Harness achieved 48.6% accuracy in online text classification, outperforming state-of-the-art hand-designed methods (ACE) by 7.7 points while using 4x fewer context tokens.

A single discovered retrieval harness improved accuracy on 200 IMO-level math problems by an average of 4.7 points across five held-out models (e.g., GPT-5.4n, Gemini-3F) compared to a 'No Retriever' baseline.
The system automatically discovered harnesses that achieved top-tier pass rates on TerminalBench-2, reaching 76.4% with Claude Opus 4.6 (ranking #2) and 37.6% with Claude Haiku 4.5 (ranking #1).
solution: Meta-Harness implements an outer-loop optimization procedure where an agentic proposer (a coding LLM) searches over task-specific harnesses.
The proposer has selective, uncompressed access to a growing filesystem containing the source code, detailed execution traces, and evaluation scores of all prior candidate harnesses.
The agent iteratively inspects this comprehensive history, reasons about failure modes, and proposes new harness candidates by modifying the Python code directly.
summary.summary: Meta-Harness provides an end-to-end optimization framework for LLM harnesses, the external code that dictates how models interact with their environment. The system utilizes an agentic proposer with filesystem access to uncompressed historical code and execution traces, leading to a 7.7-point accuracy improvement in text classification, a 4.7-point average gain in math reasoning, and competitive pass rates on agentic coding benchmarks.

title: Meta-Harness: End-to-End Optimization of Model Harnesses
